Schedule Notification by Chairperson under Section 13
CHAPTER 7
AGRICULTURE (HURRICANE REHABILITATION) ACT
An Act to authorise the Minister to make advances to owners of agricultural
properties damaged or destroyed by hurricane.

[16th November, 1955.]
1. Short title
This Act may be cited as the Agriculture (Hurricane Rehabilitation) Act.
2. Interpretation
In this Act—
“agricultural land” includes—
(a) all land used for agriculture which is so used for the purpose of a trade or
business;
(b) all land held, directly or indirectly, under or by virtue of a grant, lease, or
licence from the Government or the Crown the terms and conditions of
which restrict the user of the land to, or require the user of the land for,
agriculture:
Provided that this expression shall not extend to land used as pleasure
grounds or private gardens;
“agriculture” means cultivation of land and the use of land for any purpose of
husbandry, and includes horticulture, fruit growing, seed growing, dairy farming
and livestock breeding and keeping, the use of land as grazing land, meadow land,
market gardens or nursery grounds, and the use of land for woodlands when that
use is ancillary to the use of land for other agricultural purposes, and “agricultural”
shall be construed accordingly;
“chairperson” means the chairperson of any committee appointed by the
Minister to administer relief under this Act;
“land” includes all land specified in the application of an owner or occupier for an
advance under this Act and all buildings, factories and machinery erected and
standing thereon belonging to the owner or occupier and used and worked in
connection with such land damaged or destroyed by hurricane;
“machinery” includes tanks, pipes, and equipment of every kind, whether
connected or not to any motor power used for the purposes of, or in connection
with, the use of the land;
“owner” means the owner of land or the duly authorised attorney of such owner,
and includes an executor or administrator of a deceased owner, a trustee appointed
by deed or will, a receiver appointed by an order of the High Court, or by a
mortgagee in possession, and also a company or corporation;
“prescribed” means prescribed by regulations under this Act;
“Registrar” means the Registrar of the Supreme Court;
“relevant circumstances”, in relation to any owner or occupier, includes all
circumstances affecting management or farming other than the personal
circumstances of the owner.
3. Agricultural areas
(1) The Minister shall for the purpose of carrying into effect the provisions of this
Act—
(a) divide Grenada into agricultural areas;
(b) appoint a panel of persons from among whom referees may be selected
for the purposes of this Act;

(c) appoint Agricultural Area Committees, (hereinafter called “area
committees”) for the purposes of this Act and such other purposes as shall
be prescribed by regulations;
(d) appoint a Central Agricultural Rehabilitation Committee (hereinafter called
the Central Committee) in the manner hereinafter provided.
(2) The persons appointed under paragraph (b) of the preceding subsection shall
not be Government employees and shall possess some reasonable experience in and a
practical working knowledge of agriculture.
(3) An area committee shall consist of a chairperson, and not less than two other
persons selected by the Governor-General and shall exercise such functions as are
conferred thereon by this Act or regulations made thereunder. The chairperson of an
area committee shall be appointed by the Minister:
Provided that in his or her absence the Central Committee may appoint some
other member to perform the duties of chairperson.
4. Powers of Agricultural Area Committee
(1) An area committee appointed under this Act shall have such powers, rights and
privileges as are vested in a magistrate under the Magistrates Act, Chapter 177, on the
occasion of any hearing, in respect of the following matters—
(a) enforcing the attendance of witnesses and examining them on oath or
affirmation;
(b) issuing summonses under the hand of the chairperson;
(c) authorising the representation before them of any person appearing to
them to be interested, by counsel, or solicitor, or otherwise if they
consider that any injustice would ensue if that person were not so
represented.
(2) The procedure at a hearing before an area committee, the remuneration of
witnesses for attendance thereat and all questions incidental to the hearing shall be in
accordance with the practice and procedure of a magistrate’s court in civil proceedings
so far as the same are applicable.
5. Central Committee
(1) The Central Committee appointed under this Act shall consist of a chairperson,
the Attorney-General, the Permanent Secretary (Finance) and the Chief Technical
Officer (Extension) and not less than five other persons, who shall have knowledge of
and experience in the practice of agriculture in Grenada:
Provided that the Chief Technical Officer (Extension) shall not take part in any
proceedings of the Central Committee under section 18.
(2) The Minister shall appoint the chairperson and may appoint any person to
assist him or her in the performance of any of his or her duties, or to act in the place
of the chairperson or of any other member of the Central Committee in case of the
absence or inability to act of the chairperson or other member.
(3) Any member of the Central Committee not being a public officer may at any
time in writing addressed to the chairperson resign his or her place on the Central
Committee and the chairperson shall forthwith cause such resignation to be forwarded
to the Minister and from the date of the receipt by the chairperson of such resignation
such member shall cease to be a member of the Central Committee.
(4) Five members of the Central Committee shall form a quorum and every
question before the Central Committee shall be decided by a majority of votes of the
members present.
6. Appointment of officers and inspectors

The Central Committee may appoint and employ at such remuneration and on such
terms and conditions as the Minister may determine, such officers and inspectors as
may be deemed necessary for the proper carrying out of the provisions of this Act and
may require any such officer or inspector so appointed to give such security as may be
considered proper for the due execution of his or her duties.
7. Expenses to be a charge on the Consolidated Fund
The expenses incurred including the remuneration (if any) of referees, officers and
inspectors, in carrying out the provisions of this Act shall be a charge on the
Consolidated Fund.
8. Applications
(1) Any owner or occupier of land may, within such time as may be prescribed by
regulations, make application for advances under this Act. Every such application shall
be in a form prescribed and shall be accompanied by recommendations of two referees
whose names are included in the panel of persons for the time being appointed under
section 3(1)(b) or of an inspector and by statutory declaration verifying the particulars
stated in the application and shall be addressed to the chairperson of the Central
Committee through the chairperson of the area committee. All recommendations by
referees or inspectors shall be treated by the committee concerned as confidential.
(2) The appropriate committee shall consider each application on its merits and in
its absolute discretion may grant or refuse loans or may grant a loan on such
conditions and on such security, in addition to the conditions and to the security
contained in this Act, as it may think fit, and shall certify upon the application the
maximum amount to be allowed, the conditions on which and the times at which such
total amount or portion thereof shall be advanced.
9. Interest
Advances made under this Act shall bear interest at such yearly rate, commencing
on such date, as may be prescribed.
10. Repayment
Advances together with any interest thereon at the prescribed rate shall be
repayable in such manner and over such period of years as may be prescribed.
11. Purpose of advance
(1) Advances may be made to any owner or occupier of any land at the discretion
of the Central Committee for the following purposes only, namely—
(a) the re-establishment of agricultural cultivations damaged or destroyed by
hurricane by replanting them with similar crops to those destroyed or by
interplanting them with secondary crops or by replanting them or a
portion thereof with other crops;
(b) the maintenance and improvement of the land, including the payment of
labour and the purchase of supplies required to plant, cultivate and
manure such land and to reap and make merchantable the crop or
produce thereof;
(c) the payment of rates and taxes on lands and buildings under the
provisions of the Real Property Tax Act, Chapter 276;
(d) the payment of premiums on insurance in so far as such insurance relates
to the production of the crop and to the buildings, factories and machinery
erected and standing on the land and used in connection with such land;
(e) the payment of interest accruing on encumbrances postponed by the
operation of this Act;

(f) the payment of subsistence allowance to the occupier when the Central
Committee is satisfied that he or she has no other means of subsistence.
(2) Every owner or occupier to whom advances have been made under this section
shall observe the following conditions, namely—
(a) he or she shall expend such advance exclusively in planting, restoring,
preserving or maintaining the crop in respect of which the advance was
made, on the land specified in his or her application or for other purposes
particularly approved by the Central Committee;
(b) he or she shall repay such advance, together with interest thereon at the
rate and at the times prescribed in the regulations and shall pay all
expenses of enforcement and realisation incurred in connection with or
arising out of the advance;
(c) he or she shall produce at such intervals as may be required by the
Central Committee or by any person thereunto authorised in writing by
the central committee, an account showing the manner in which the
advance was expended, and such amount may be required to be verified
on oath or in such other manner as may be prescribed;
(d) he or she shall at all times allow inspection, by any person thereunto
authorised in writing by the Central Committee, of the land in respect of
which the advance was made;
(e) he or she shall at all times uphold and maintain the cultivation on the land
in respect of which the advance was made so that the security for the
advance does not deteriorate in value.
12. Vouchers for advances
(1) It shall be lawful for the Minister to authorise the Accountant-General to pay
out of the Consolidated Fund such sums as shall have been certified to have been
allowed by the Central Committee on such terms as may be provided as may be
necessary to meet any expenditure duly incurred in the carrying out of the provisions
of this Act.
(2) The chairperson of the Central Committee shall forward to the Accountant-
General the vouchers certifying the amounts allowed, and, upon the receipt thereof,
the Accountant-General shall, under the authority of the warrant of the Minister, pay
out of the Consolidated Fund such sums as have been allowed by the committee
concerned:
Provided that no such vouchers shall be certified until the expiration of three
days from the time of filing by the Registrar of the notification referred to in section
13, and that where the Central Committee has delegated authority to an area
committee, the chairperson of the Central Committee may with the approval of the
Permanent Secretary (Finance) authorise payment to the area committee of a block
amount to meet individual payments allowed by the area committee.
13. Notification to Registrar of advances allowed
As soon as possible after the fixing of the maximum advances to be allowed to any
person under section 8(2), the chairperson of the Central Committee shall forward to
the Registrar a notification in the form prescribed in the Schedule containing the
necessary particulars and upon receipt thereof the Registrar shall thereupon file the
same in a register to be kept for the purpose, and such register shall be open to
inspection by the public free of cost.
14. Charge in respect of loans
Subject to the provisions of this Act—
(a) Owners.—Any advance made to an owner of land for the purposes of this
Act, together with interest thereon and all costs, charges and expenses

properly incurred preparatory or incidental to the enforcement of the
security, shall until repayment in full constitutes a charge upon the land to
which the advance relates, ranking after any encumbrances, estates,
interests and rights affecting such land subsisting or outstanding at the
date when a notification in respect of the advance was filed in accordance
with section 13 and entered before that date in the records of the
Supreme Court or duly registered before that date in the Deeds and Land
Registry, but before all other encumbrances, estates, interests and rights
affecting such land subsisting or outstanding at such date:
Provided that no steps or proceedings towards sale of land, foreclosure or
forfeiture shall be commenced until the expiration of thirty days after
service upon the Attorney-General of notice of intention to proceed to the
sale of land, foreclosure or forfeiture by the person on whose behalf such
steps or proceedings are taken:
Provided further, however, that in the case of any judgement entered as
aforesaid or other encumbrance in relation whereto the covenant for
repayment of principal is for a period not exceeding six months from the
date thereof, no steps or proceedings towards sale of land, foreclosure or
forfeiture shall be commenced until the expiration of one year from
service upon the Attorney-General of notice of intention to sell, foreclose
or effect a forfeiture unless—
(i) interest is in arrears, or
(ii) the consent of the Attorney-General is obtained;
(b) Occupiers.—Any advance made to an occupier for the purposes of this Act,
together with interest thereon and all costs, charges and expenses
properly incurred preparatory or incidental to the enforcement of the
security shall, notwithstanding any prior encumbrance, estate, interest or
right in the crops or land, be a first and preferential charge on the crops in
respect of which the advance was made, growing or to be grown on the
land specified in the notification—
(i) if the loan was granted with the approval of the owner, until
repayment in full of all sums charged upon the crops,
(ii) if the loan was not granted with the approval of the owner, until
repayment in full of all sums charged upon the crops, so however
that such charge shall not subsist for longer than the period of the
tenant’s interest in the land at the time of the application, and in
either such case such crops on severance from the land shall not be
deemed to be personal chattels within the meaning of the Bills of
Sale Act, Chapter 33.
The approval referred to in subparagraph (i) of this paragraph shall be given by the
owner to the Central Committee by endorsement on the form of application prescribed
or otherwise in writing addressed to the Central Committee and shall be endorsed on
the notification.
15. Priority of advances
Notwithstanding anything in any Act, or any provision of law or equity, to the
contrary immediately upon the making of the advances authorised by this Act with the
previous consent of the encumbrancer, if any, first had and obtained, all persons
dealing with the land or any interest therein shall be deemed to have notice of such
advances to the limit stated to be secured in the notification, and all such dealings
shall be and be construed to be subject to the charge and priority created by this Act.
16. Misapplication of advance
If any person, and in the case of a company or corporation, the person in charge or
control of the land, shall apply any advances made under the provisions of this Act to
any purpose other than those specified in or allowed under this Act he or she shall be

guilty of an offence and liable, on summary conviction, to a fine of three thousand
dollars and to imprisonment for six months.
17. Inspection
Any inspector appointed by the Central Committee may at all reasonable times
enter upon any land in respect of which an advance has been made under the
provisions of this Act and inspect the same; and any person obstructing or causing to
be obstructed any inspector in making such inspection, whether by force or by threats
or in any other manner, shall be guilty of an offence and liable, on summary
conviction, to a fine of three thousand dollars and to imprisonment for three months.
18. Duties of good management and husbandry
(1) The provisions of this Act shall have effect for the purpose of securing that
owners and occupiers of agricultural land fulfil their responsibilities to the community
to manage and farm land so as to restore the agricultural industry to a condition
comparable with that obtaining before a hurricane.
(2) For the purposes of this Act, an owner of agricultural land shall be deemed to
have fulfilled his or her responsibilities to the community as mentioned in the
preceding subsection, if the management of the land, by him or her is such as to be
reasonably adequate, having regard to the character and situation of the land and
other relevant circumstances, to enable an occupier of the land reasonably skilled in
husbandry to re-establish, rehabilitate and maintain agricultural cultivations destroyed
or damaged by hurricane.
(3) In determining whether the owner or occupier of agricultural land is fulfilling
his or her responsibilities as aforesaid, regard shall be had to the character and
situation of the land and the extent to which steps are being taken to—
(a) terrace sloping land under cultivation;
(b) prevent or check the formation of gullies;
(c) prevent surface water from causing erosion on adjoining land;
(d) protect water courses or public roads;
(e) preserve forests and vegetation on ridges and steep slopes;
(f) control or where necessary to prevent the grazing of animals on steep
slopes;
(g) restore and by more intensive and better selective cultivation increase the
productive capacity of agricultural land damaged or destroyed by
hurricane;
(h) render the restoration of permanent benefit by inducing better land use
and planned cultivation.
(4) Where the Chief Technical Officer (Extension) is satisfied that the owner or
occupier of agricultural land is not fulfilling his or her responsibilities as aforesaid, the
Chief Technical Officer (Extension) may apply to the Central Committee to make an
order (hereinafter referred to as a “supervision order”) placing the owner or occupier
of the land under the supervision of the Chief Technical Officer (Extension) in so far as
it relates respectively to the management and farming of the land in question.
(5) Before a supervision order is made in pursuance of the preceding subsection
the Central Committee shall afford the owner or occupier of the said land an
opportunity of being heard in opposition to the application of the Chief Technical
Officer (Extension), and for this purpose shall refer the matter to an area committee to
examine the said application and shall inform the owner or occupier accordingly.
(6) If an owner or occupier does not appear at the time and place notified to him
or her for the hearing of the application, or on any adjournment thereof granted by the

area committee, the committee may on proof of service of a summons or notice on
him or her requiring his or her attendance, hear the application in his or her absence.
(7) The area committee concerned having taken into consideration the
representations made to it in pursuance of the provisions of subsection (5) shall
submit a report embodying such representations and their decisions thereon to the
Central Committee.
(8) Upon receipt of a report from the area committee pursuant to subsection (7),
the Central Committee shall notify the Chief Technical Officer (Extension) and the
owner or occupier of the land concerned.
(9) Within ten days of the receipt of such notice, the Chief Technical Officer
(Extension) or the owner or occupier concerned may request the Central Committee to
review the decision of the area committee.
(10) The Central Committee shall thereupon review the said decision by way of re-
hearing and for this purpose the Central Committee shall have the same powers as are
conferred on an area committee by section 4.
(11) If, having reviewed the decision of the area committee, the Central
Committee is of opinion that a supervision order should be made, it shall thereupon
make such an order against the owner or occupier concerned.
19. Duties and powers of Chief Technical Officer (Extension) under a
supervision order
(1) While a supervision order is in force, the Chief Technical Officer (Extension) or
any of his or her officers authorised by him or her in writing in that behalf may at all
reasonable times enter upon the land to which the order relates, for the purpose of
inspecting the way in which it is being managed and farmed.
(2) Where a supervision order is in force and the Central Committee is satisfied
that by reason of the standard of management and husbandry, as the case may be,
attained by the person to whom it relates it is no longer necessary that the order
should continue in force, the Central Committee shall revoke the order:
Provided that the revocation of an order shall not affect any direction given
thereunder in so far as it is in force immediately before the revocation of the order.
(3) Forthwith after the making of a supervision order, the chairperson of the
Central Committee shall cause a copy of the said order to be served upon the person
to whom it relates; and forthwith after the revocation of such an order the chairperson
of the Central Committee shall cause notice of such revocation to be served upon the
person to whom it relates.
20. Supervision order not affected by change of owner
Where a supervision order is in force in respect of an owner or occupier, any
disposition of land to which the order relates, whereby some other person becomes the
owner or occupier of that land, shall not affect the continued operation of the
supervision order and the said order shall continue in force so far as it relates to that
land as if it had been made so as to relate to the new owner or occupier, as the case
may be, as well as to the former owner or occupier.
21. Directions to secure good management of land to avoid soil erosion
(1) When a supervision order is in force, the Chief Technical Officer (Extension),
after affording to the person to whom the order relates an opportunity of making
representations to him or her, whether in writing or on being heard by the Chief
Technical Officer (Extension) or a person appointed by him or her, may by notice in
writing served on the person to whom the order relates give to that person such
directions as the Chief Technical Officer (Extension) is satisfied are required—

(a) where the order is for the supervision of the management of the land, to
secure that the said person fulfils his or her responsibilities to manage the
land in accordance with the rules of good estate management;
(b) where the order is for the supervision of the farming of an agricultural
unit, to secure that the said person fulfils his or her responsibilities to
farm the unit in accordance with the rules of good husbandry.
(2) Such directions may impose requirements, restrictions or prohibitions as to the
carrying out of work and as to the manner in which the land is to be used.
(3) If any person to whom a direction is given under this section contravenes the
direction, he or she shall be guilty of an offence and liable, on summary conviction, to
a fine of one thousand dollars and, in default of payment, to imprisonment for three
months.
22. Release of charge on repayment of advances
Any person may at any time repay the whole or any part of the amount outstanding
in respect of any advance and on the repayment by any person of advances made to
him or her under this Act and of all interest payable thereon, the Accountant-General
shall give a certificate to that effect, and thereupon the charge created by this Act in
respect of such advances and interest shall be released, and the Registrar shall, upon
the production to him or her of such certificate, endorse on the notification filed by him
or her a memorandum to the effect that such charge has been released, and the
Registrar shall sign such memorandum.
23. Rendering of accounts
If the Central Committee is dissatisfied with the management of the land by reason
of persistent failure to comply with the provisions of this Act or regulations made
thereunder by a person in receipt of advances (three convictions under the Act being
prima facie evidence of such persistent failure) or if any such person is in default for a
period exceeding six months with regard to any requirements of this Act or regulations
made thereunder for repayment of principal or interest, the Central Committee may
recommend to the Minister that the account should be closed and the Minister may on
such recommendation of the Central Committee direct the Accountant-General to
make up an account showing the amounts advanced to the person together with
interest thereon and deliver to such person a copy of such account. The account shall
be rendered to such person either personally or by sending it by post to him or her to
his or her registered address or to the address supplied by him or her to the
committee for the giving of notices to him or her. When a notice is sent by post,
service of the notice shall be deemed to be effected by properly addressing and
posting a letter containing the notice, and unless the contrary is proved, to have been
effected at the time at which the letter would be delivered in the ordinary course of the
post.
24. Enforcement of security of owner by sale
If any owner to whom an advance has been made under this Act—
(a) defaults in the payment of any monies payable by him or her under this
Act; or
(b) fails to observe any of the conditions specified in section 11 or any of the
additional conditions on which any advance has been made by the Central
Committee,
the Central Committee shall have the power to order the sale of all or any part of the
land specified in the notification, and of the buildings and machinery erected and
standing thereon belonging to the owner and used and worked in connection with such
land, subject to all encumbrances, estates, interests and rights affecting such land
subsisting or outstanding at the date when the notification in respect of the advance
was filed and still subsisting or outstanding.

25. Application of principal money after sale
(1) All money received from the sale of any land, buildings and machinery under
section 24 shall be applied—
(a) first, towards all costs, charges and expenses properly incurred and
incidental to the sale;
(b) secondly, towards satisfying any encumbrances, estates, interests and
rights in or over any such land, buildings and machinery which rank before
the amounts charged by this Act thereon to which the sale has not been
made subject, in order of their priority;
(c) thirdly, towards payment of any amounts owing in respect of advances
and interests and charged upon the land under this Act; and
(d) finally, the residue, if any, shall be paid to the person legally entitled to
give a discharge therefor.
(2) Where the amount derived from any sale under section 24 is insufficient fully
to discharge the indebtedness of the borrower in respect of advances and interest
thereon, the borrower shall be liable to pay the balance of such indebtedness forthwith
upon demand by the Accountant-General.
26. Enforcement of security by possession
(1) If any occupier to whom an advance is granted under this Act—
(a) (i) defaults in the payment of any monies payable by him or her under
this Act, or
(ii) fails to observe any of the conditions specified in section 11 or any
of the additional conditions on which any advance is made by the
Central Committee; and
(b) is reaping or is about to reap the crops the subject of the charge created
by section 14,
the Central Committee shall have power to take immediate possession of such crops
and, so far as may be necessary for the purpose of reaping such crops, of all lands on
which such crops are grown and after such reaping to sell such crops either by public
auction or by private treaty as the Central Committee may elect.
(2) Where the occupier does not on demand by the Central Committee deliver up
immediate possession of the crops and of the land on which such crops are growing to
the Central Committee, the Central Committee may cause the secretary of the
committee to file in the magistrate’s court in the district in which the land is situate an
affidavit, to which shall be exhibited a copy of the notification, stating the sum owing
on account of the advance for principal and interest and—
(a) (i) that default has been made in the payment of any monies payable
under this Act, or
(ii) that there has been a failure to observe any of the conditions
specified in section 11 or any of the additional conditions on which
the loan was granted by the Central Committee; and
(b) that the occupier is reaping or about to reap the crops the subject of the
charge created by section 14,
and thereupon the magistrate may grant to the Central Committee a warrant of
possession of such crops and of such land so far as may be necessary for the purpose
of reaping such crops.
27. Application of proceeds from sale of crops
(1) All monies received from the sale of any crops after possession has been
obtained under section 26 shall be applied—

(a) first, towards all costs, charges and expenses properly incurred and
incidental to obtaining possession of such crops, to reaping them, and to
the sale thereof;
(b) secondly, towards payment of all amounts owing by the occupier, as the
case may be, in respect of advances and interest, and charged upon the
crops; and
(c) finally, the residue, if any, shall be paid to the person legally entitled to
give a discharge therefor.
(2) Where the amount derived from any sale under section 26 is insufficient fully
to discharge the indebtedness of the borrower under this Act, the borrower shall be
liable to pay the balance of such indebtedness forthwith upon demand by the
Accountant-General.
28. Right of encumbrancer
Where any account has been delivered and a sale has been ordered by the Central
Committee under the provisions of sections 23 and 24, it shall be lawful for any
encumbrancer of the land or part thereof ordered to be sold to pay to the Accountant-
General the amount shown in such account or such other amount as on the day of
payment shall be a charge on the said land, and, upon such payment being made, the
Accountant-General shall give a receipt for the same and issue a certificate to the
effect that all advances made under this Act have been repaid, and the Registrar shall,
upon the production to him or her of such certificate, endorse on the notification filed
by him or her a memorandum to the effect that payment has been duly made by such
encumbrancer, and thereupon the charge created by this Act shall be deemed to be
transferred to and shall vest in such encumbrancer subject to all estates, interests,
rights and easements affecting the same to which, had the land or part thereof been
sold by order of the Minister under the provisions of the last preceding section, it
would have been subject, and such encumbrancer shall have, in respect of the amount
paid, all the powers of a mortgagee where the mortgage is by deed and where the
mortgage money has become due:
Provided that the right hereby conferred upon encumbrancers shall accrue to
each encumbrancer, but such right shall, as between encumbrancers accrue to a prior
encumbrancer before any subsequent encumbrancer.
29. Power to vary order of sale
Where the Central Committee shall have made any order for sale under the
provisions of section 24, the Central Committee may, at any time before the sale,
vary, amend or revise such order, and may make such further order as the Central
Committee may think fit.
30. Sale to be by public auction
(1) Every sale made in pursuance of the power of sale conferred by this Act shall
be by public auction at the Registrar’s Office. Notice of such sale shall be given by
advertisement appearing in the Gazette and in one or more local newspapers and
published at least once in each of three consecutive weeks before the day of such sale:
Provided always that the Minister may, at any time before the sale of any land so
advertised, postpone the sale thereof, either generally or to some specified day.
(2) Whenever any land advertised for sale is not sold on the date appointed for
sale thereof it shall be lawful for the Minister to order that the land be again put up for
sale at the last reserve price reduced by ten per cent and in such case notice of the
postponed sale shall be given by public advertisement in the manner provided in
subsection (1).
(3) The Government shall be at liberty to bid at any sale made in pursuance of this
section.

31. Conveyance by Minister and application of purchase money
Whenever any land or any part thereof shall have been sold by public auction under
the provisions of this Act, the Minister shall convey the same by deed to the purchaser
and the land, or the part thereof as the case may be, therein described, shall become
the property of the purchaser absolutely, freed and discharged from all encumbrances,
save and except any charges thereon in respect of any debts due to Her Majesty but
subject to any estates, interests, rights and easements affecting the same to which the
sale has been subjected:
Sale to encumbrancer.—Provided that where any land or any part thereof subject
to any encumbrance is sold to the encumbrancer thereof it shall be lawful for the
Minister to convey or transfer the same to such encumbrancer in the manner
hereinbefore provided, upon payment of so much only of the purchase money as
exceeds the amount due under the charge created by this Act, and thereupon the land
or the part thereof so sold shall become the property of such encumbrancer freed and
discharged from all encumbrances, save and except any charge thereon in respect of
any debts due to Her Majesty, and save and except the charge created by this Act and
the amount due thereunder, but subject to any estate, interests, rights or easements
affecting the same to which the sale has been made subject. In every such case the
part of the purchase money paid by the encumbrancer as aforesaid shall be deposited
with the Accountant-General and shall be held by him or her in trust for
encumbrancers (if any) in order of priority or any other person legally entitled to give
a discharge for the same; and the balance of such purchase money, representing the
amount due under the charge created by this Act, shall be deemed to have been
advanced to such encumbrancer under the provisions of this Act and shall be
recoverable in the manner provided by this Act.
32. Release of land charged under this Act
(1) Any owner who shall be desirous of selling any portion of the land charged
under this Act, freed and discharged from the charge created by this Act, may apply to
the Minister for permission to do so, and the Minister in the case of land intended to be
sold may, if satisfied that the remaining part of the land so charged is a sufficient
security for the amount advanced to the owner under the authority of this Act, and for
any interest payable thereon, or for the unpaid parts thereof, respectively, by deed
release the part of the land intended to be sold from the charge by this Act, and the
Registrar shall, upon production of such deed to him or her for such purpose, endorse
on the notification filed with him or her under section 13 and relating to the portion of
the land intended to be sold a memorandum to the effect that the portion of the land
comprised in such deed has been released from the charge created under this Act. In
the case of land intended to be leased or otherwise dealt with the Minister may grant
permission to lease or otherwise deal with such land or any portion thereof upon such
conditions as he or she may think fit:
Provided that the Minister shall not release any land intended to be sold or
impose conditions relating to land intended to be leased or otherwise dealt with as
aforesaid unless with the consent in writing of any encumbrancer to whom such owner
may be indebted in respect of any encumbrances held by him or her at the time of the
creation of any charge under this Act.
(2) Every such application as referred to in the last preceding subsection shall be
in writing and shall set forth—
(a) full particulars of the land intended to be sold, leased or otherwise dealt
with;
(b) the purchase price, rent, or other consideration to be paid or reserved on
such sale, lease, or other dealing;
(c) the name and address of the intended purchaser or lessee or the person
with whom any other dealing is intended to be effected;
(d) the amount, if any, which may be owing by the applicant to any
encumbrancer;
(e) the name and address of such encumbrancer,

and every such application shall be accompanied by a statutory declaration verifying
the matters set forth in such application.
33. Protection of trustees
No concurrence or acquiescence, whether past or future, on the part of any trustee
in the granting of any advance under the provisions of this Act shall of itself be
deemed improper or a breach of trust so as to render such trustee liable in respect of
any loss or consequence resulting from such advance having been granted, any rule of
law or equity to the contrary notwithstanding.
34. Protection of agricultural contracts
Nothing in this Act shall affect the priority of contracts or the rights of contractors
under the Agricultural Small Tenancies Act, Chapter 10.
35. Registration of conveyance on sale
A deed executed by the Minister in exercise of the power of sale conferred by this
Act shall be entered, on the delivery thereof to the Registrar, in the register of deeds.
36. Provision relating to statutory declarations
Every statutory declaration required under the provisions of this Act to accompany
an application under this Act shall be made by the applicant or his or her duly
authorised attorney. No fee shall be chargeable in respect of any such statutory
declaration.
37. Fees
Notwithstanding anything contained in this Act, for all matters and things done by
any person or any committee in connection with an advance under the provisions of
this Act or the repayment thereof, the Registrar shall be entitled to such fees only as
may be prescribed.
38. Exemption from stamp duty
An instrument creating a charge under this Act shall be exempt from stamp duty.
39. Limit of time for prosecution
Proceedings in respect of offences against this Act may be commenced at any time
within two years from the date on which the offence was committed.
40. Regulations
The Minister may make regulations for all or any of the following purposes—
(a) regulating the administration of relief under this Act and carrying out the
provisions of this Act;
(b) prescribing the limits within which advances may be made under this Act;
(c) prescribing the form in which and the period within which applications for
advances under this Act may be made;
(d) prescribing the conditions upon which advances may be made under this
Act;
(e) prescribing the rate of interest to be charged on advances made under
this Act;

(f) prescribing the period of years within which advances made under this Act
shall be repayable and the manner of repayment;
(g) prescribing the fees to be paid and the manner in which they may be paid
in respect of any matter or thing to be done in connection with advances
made under this Act;
(h) prescribing a scale of legal charges for investigation of title to property;
(i) generally providing for any matters which are authorised by this Act to be
prescribed.

AGRICULTURE (HURRICANE REHABILITATION) REGULATIONS
[Section 40.]
[10th March, 1956.]
1. Citation
These Regulations may be cited as the Agriculture (Hurricane Rehabilitation)
Regulations.
2. Definition of Areas
For the purposes of these Regulations, the State of Grenada shall be divided into
twelve Areas as follows—
Area I ................. That portion of the parish of Saint George North of the Grand Etang
Road.
Area II ................. The parish of Saint John.
Area III ................. The parish of Saint Mark.
Area IV ................. That portion of the parish of Saint Patrick West of the Saint Patrick
River from the sea and West of the Great Arm of the Saint Patrick
River in its upper reaches.
Area V ................. That portion of the parish of Saint Patrick East of Area IV described
above.
Area VI ................. That portion of the Parish of Saint Andrew North of the Great River
and of the Great Branch of the said river in its upper reaches.
Area VII ................. That portion of the Parish of Saint Andrew South of Area VI described
above, and North of the Grand Etang, St. James, Grenville Road.
Area VIII ................. That portion of the parish of Saint Andrew lying South of the Grand
Etang, St. James, Grenville Road.
Area IX ................. That portion of the parish of Saint David East of the main stream of
the river of Little Marquis or La Sagesse River.
Area X ................. That portion of the parish of Saint David lying West of Area IX
described above.
Area XI ................. That portion of the parish of Saint George South of the Grand Etang
Road.
Area XII ................. The District of Carriacou comprising the Grenadines dependencies of
Grenada.
3. Consolidation of Areas
(1) From and after a date to be notified by the Minister by notice published in the
Gazette, the agricultural areas appearing in the first column hereunder shall be
consolidated into the areas named in the second column—
First Column Second Column
Area I
Area II Western District
Area III
Area IV
Northern District
Area IX
Area X
Southern District
Area XI

(2) Upon the date or dates notified in accordance with this Regulation, the Area
Committee appointed in respect of the areas in the first column in the preceding
paragraph shall be dissolved and the Area Committee appointed in respect of the areas
set out in the second column shall have and exercise all the functions, powers and
duties of the Committees so dissolved and shall as far as practicable for all purposes
stand in the place of the said Committees.
(3) The Minister may notify different dates for the consolidation of the respective
areas.
4. Purposes of Loan
Loans shall be made for the re-establishment of agricultural cultivations, namely
for—
(a) clearing and replanting;
(b) cocoa rehabilitation;
(c) nutmeg rehabilitation;
(d) rehabilitation of coconuts and other long term crops;
(e) banana rehabilitation and extension;
(f) farm recovery, with special reference to short term crops;
(g) purchasing implements, supplies, manures, etc.;
(h) repairs to farm buildings, excluding estate housing;
(i) restoring roads, dams, canals, wind belts, drainage, soil conservation and
general restoration of a permanent nature;
(j) other purposes which fall within section 11 of the Act.
5. Rehabilitation Fund
The Rehabilitation Fund shall be constituted as follows—
(a) Fund A.—The sum of $480,000 and, in addition, such sums as may be
added thereto to facilitate a transition from emergency to long term
rehabilitation.
(b) Fund B.—The remainder of loan funds for agricultural rehabilitation to be
made available to farmers on the basis of individual rehabilitation
programmes on both long and short term.
6. Security for Loans
Loans shall be made—
(a) on the security of agricultural land held for an estate of freehold;
(b) on the security of leasehold land held for a term of years on tenure
acceptable to the Central Committee in its discretion or supported by the
acceptance of the owner of a charge on the freehold;
(c) on the collateral security of lands, other than those to be rehabilitated, or
of other property including investments;
(d) in the case of lands forming part of Government Land Settlements, with
the written approval of the Minister signified by the Land Settlement
Officer;
(e) to occupiers of agricultural small tenancies within the framework of
producer associations.

7. Emergency Fund
(1) Fund “A” shall constitute an Emergency Fund for the early restoration of
cultivations damaged by the hurricane. The principal uses are to meet the labour costs
of clearing and repair of damaged cultivations, the purchase of essential equipment
and material and repairs to essential farm buildings. The loan shall be free of interest
for three years from the date of the filing of a notification with the Registrar in respect
thereof and thereafter shall bear interest at two per cent per annum and shall be
repayable over a period of five further years.
(2) Fund “A” shall be made available in advance of the dry season to encourage
maximum rehabilitation while the rains continue.
(3) Advances shall be made upon estimates of fortnightly wage bills and subject,
after first payments, to certificates of two referees, that work to the value of the first
advance has been performed.
(4) The Central Committee may entertain applications in respect of work
performed in anticipation of a loan.
(5) Should favourable weather conditions continue beyond the period in respect of
which second instalments have been paid, on presentation of claims for work further
performed, certified as aforesaid by two referees, the Central Committee may sanction
further fortnightly advances in each case not exceeding advances previously made for
any preceding fortnightly period on notification to the Registrar supplemented
accordingly.
(6) After the 31st day of January, 1956, no payments in respect of applications for
first advances from Fund “A” shall be made:
Provided that such applications as may be submitted shall after that date be
considered for approval under the terms of Fund “B” if the applicant so desires.
(7) The 4th day of February, 1956, is declared to be the terminal date for the
purpose of re-imbursement for work done in the emergency period.
(8) The Central Committee may accept for payment under the terms of the
Emergency Fund, expenditure certified to have been incurred in accordance with
subregulation (5) of this Regulation up to and including the terminal date, and any
payments in excess of Fund “A” made in re-imbursement of expenditure so incurred
shall be on the same terms as to repayment and interest as the initial Emergency Fund
advances.
(9) Where an encumbrancer has consented to an application for an advance under
Fund “A”, notwithstanding the provisions of section 14 of the Act, the charge in respect
of such advance shall be accepted as ranking pari passu with the security constituted
by the encumbrance.
8. Long term loan
(1) Fund “B” shall constitute the long term loan fund and loans therefrom shall be
free of interest for five years from the date of filing with the Registrar a notification in
respect thereof and shall bear interest thereafter at the rate of three and a half per
cent per annum repayable over a period of twenty further years.
(2) Application from Fund “B” shall be in the form contained in the Schedule.
(3) The final date for the submission of applications under Scheme “B” shall be as
determined by the Central Committee.
(4) Any unexpended balance of Fund “A” after the 31st day of January, 1956, shall
be applied first to the payment of second and later advances under approved loans
from Fund “A”.
(5) If any sum is necessary to be provided from Fund “B” to supplement the
balance of Fund “A” in order to satisfy advances subsequent to the second then—

(a) the sum so provided from Fund “B” shall be available on the same terms
as loans from Fund “A”; or
(b) as from a date to be fixed by the Minister any part of the sum so provided
shall be treated as an instalment against Fund “B” on the terms thereof as
to period of repayment and interest, to borrowers who wish to obtain a
loan under that Scheme.
9. Scheme B
(1) The Central Committee shall enter upon an examination of the information
relating to total rehabilitation contained in the forms of application received under the
Emergency Scheme and revise the same in the light of applications received for the
particular purposes of the long term scheme (hereinafter referred to as Scheme “B”).
(2) Applications under Scheme “B” on the prescribed form setting out in detail
particulars for long term rehabilitation shall be examined by Area Committees and
submitted to the Central Committee with recommendations.
(3) The Central Committee shall seek to establish within the limit of funds available
for the purpose, maximum advances per acre relating to each orchard crop and shall
give guidance to Area Committees thereon.
(4) The Central Committee shall decide whether, and if so, what funds may be
made available to applicants, the conditions and instalments applicable thereto, and
cause notifications to be filed accordingly with the Registrar.
(5) Pending the submission and approval of applications in the form prescribed for
Fund “B”, the Central Committee may approve on the basis of applications approved
under Fund “A” periodic payments to persons previously in receipt of advances from
Fund “A”, on presentation from such persons of claims certified as hereinafter
provided, for work performed after the terminal date.
(6) Payments under Scheme “B” shall be made upon the certificate of an Inspector
reporting directly to the Central Committee that he or she has examined work done
during any relevant period and of the value of such work.
(7) For the purpose of Scheme “B”, full time Inspectors shall be employed and
stationed in teams of three or more in districts to be appointed by the Central
Committee from which to inspect cultivations. Inspectors may be rotated as directed
by the Central Committee. With respect to Government land settlements and Area XII,
in other words the Carriacou District, officers of the Ministry of Agriculture shall be
Inspectors ex officio.
(8) The Committee shall inform the Chief Technical Officer (Extension) whenever it
has reason to believe that rehabilitation funds are not being spent for the proper
purposes or with satisfactory results.
10. Short term loans
Until such time as approved loans under Scheme “B” have been fully taken up by
applicants, the Central Committee shall be empowered to issue short term loans from
the unexpended balance of Fund “B” for the purpose and on the terms and conditions
contained in the form prescribed in the Schedule with respect to short term loans.
In the application of these Regulations to Area XII, the Committee may substitute
the form specially provided therefor in the Schedule for application to Carriacou.
11. Repayment
(1) The capital amount of advances shall be repaid by such annual instalments of
principal as may be agreed between the borrower and the Central Committee—
(a) in the case of loans under Fund “A”, within a period not exceeding eight
years; and

(b) in the case of loans under Fund “B”, within a period not exceeding twenty-
five years, from the date of the notification filed with the Registrar in
respect of the said advance; and
(c) in the case of short term loans made in pursuance of these Regulations,
within periods not exceeding twelve months, eighteen months or three
years as appropriate to the purposes of such loans as set out in the form
prescribed in the Schedule with respect thereto:
Provided, however, that in default of agreement repayment shall be made
by means of equated annuities—
(i) in the case of loans under Fund “A”, commencing at a date four
years from the date of the filing of the notification with the Registrar
in respect of the advance, and
(ii) in the case of loans under Fund “B”, commencing at a date six years
from the date of the filing of the notification in manner aforesaid.
(2) The Central Committee may, on the application of the borrower where the
Committee thinks fit, vary the terms of the said agreement.

Short term loans shall be issued only for specified crops as shown in the table below, and for
purchases of and expenditure on approved items deemed necessary for the better operation of
a holding undergoing rehabilitation.
Loans for crops may be made in four equal instalments and in advance of work done or
purchases made. The issue of the second and subsequent instalments will be governed by the
satisfactory use of any previous instalment.
Loans for bananas grown for export in pure stands and for cane will be repayable within
eighteen months from the date of issue of the first instalments.
Loans for mixed crops (which may include export bananas) will be repayable within twelve
months from the date of issue of the first instalments.
Loans for machinery, appliances, fencing, livestock and other approved items will be repayable
within three years from the date of issue of loans.

Loans will be interest free but any loans becoming overdue may be charged interest at a rate
not exceeding twelve and a half per cent per annum as from the date on which the loans
become overdue.

LONG TERM
